title: Field Briefing: Similar-Looking Elements, Different Contracts
summary: Field Briefing: Similar-Looking Elements, Different Contracts retrieves prior HTML skills in an original nuanced element comparison scenario and requires source, parsed-structure, keyboard, and content evidence.
objectives: Apply semantic text meaning in unfamiliar content.
Produce or audit a real nuanced element comparison artifact without appearance-only reasoning.
Keep earlier document requirements active while verifying native behavior and valid relationships.

options.text: Nuanced element comparison case 1: use strong for every heading because bold text is always more semantic.
Nuanced element comparison case 1: select inline elements from the meaning the sentence needs, and leave purely visual treatment to CSS.
Nuanced element comparison case 1: use abbr around unfamiliar full words even when no abbreviation exists.

steps.title: Read the nuanced element comparison contract · theory
steps.instruction: Checkpoint 4: Which practice follows the explanation when styling changes? Scenario: nuanced element comparison.
steps.why: Field Briefing: Similar-Looking Elements, Different Contracts keeps meaning testable instead of depending on appearance. This is checkpoint 4 in nuanced element comparison.

content.text: Text-level elements communicate linguistic meaning. Strong marks importance, em adds stress emphasis, dfn identifies a defining instance, abbr can connect an abbreviation to its expansion, cite names a creative work, and code identifies computer-language text. Their default appearance is incidental and may change. Apply it to nuanced element comparison.

Nuanced element comparison case 4: strong and em are merely longer spellings of bold and italic, so they can style any text without changing meaning.
